metric_name: NT market penetration at time of study
metric_value: NT market penetration at 25%; new-market share at 40% and growing by some estimates
notes: Aberdeen citing industry estimates at October 1998

metric_value: NT 5.0 will not ship until late 1999 at earliest; may be delayed by 6-12 months
notes: Aberdeen's own projection; most customers will deploy gradually rather than immediately at FCS

metric_value: Windows 2000 RTM December 15 1999; GA February 17 2000 — consistent with Aberdeen's late-1999 prediction
notes: Source: Microsoft press release https://news.microsoft.com/source/1999/12/15/microsoft-releases-windows-2000-to-manufacturing/

metric_value: Should have greatest OS-upgrade impact on all types of enterprises at least since the heyday of the mainframe; and perhaps the greatest impact ever
notes: Aberdeen's headline thesis for the entire report

metric_value: NT 5.0 has as much as 40 million lines of code
notes: Based on Microsoft briefings; Aberdeen cited this as evidence of unprecedented scope

metric_value: Many immediately-needed robustness improvements folded into Service Pack 4 for NT 4.0 due October 1998; NT 5.0 will offer less added bang for some customers' bucks
notes: Aberdeen counseled deferring NT 5.0 for customers already planning SP4 deployment

metric_value: Enterprise Edition with enhanced scalability projected within a year of NT 5.0 FCS; 64-bit version planned for Intel IA-64 when Merced reaches mass market
notes: Microsoft Cluster Server requires careful testing; 64-bit apps require extensive rewriting

metric_value: NT 5.0 improvements will make it comparable to some midrange environments; but will not fully arrive until 1 year (best case) or 2 years (Aberdeen worst-case) post-FCS
notes: Aberdeen's worst-case of 2 years implied full reliability only by 2001 — consistent with actual adoption patterns

metric_value: Short-term developer productivity will go down sharply; long-term benefits from integrated directory services and enhanced file services
notes: Unprecedented complexity of NT 5.0 APIs; Aberdeen predicted steep learning curve

metric_value: ZAW reaches full stride with NT 5.0 FCS; but will not deliver zero administration without ISV tools that won't arrive until 1-2 years post-FCS
notes: MMC and IntelliMirror both require substantial customization before yielding value

metric_value: Kerberos; PKI; and SSO added to NT 5.0; but Microsoft has not shown signs of resolving heterogeneous interoperability — Aberdeen recommends other suppliers for mixed environments
notes: Mixed-environment security cited as real problem area for NT users in 1998

metric_value: Smaller orgs unaffected; mid- and large-sized organizations will need major effort to deploy NT beyond workgroup and across the enterprise via Active Directory
notes: Active Directory's problems at launch are well-documented historical fact

metric_value: Microsoft best game in town for Internet/Intranet scaling applications; NT 5.0 will strengthen but not fully deliver on Internet infrastructure promise
notes: Aberdeen judged Microsoft's ISP partnerships favorably

metric_value: NT 5.0 Workstation requires 64 MB RAM; users upgrading desktops will need to rebuild file systems — major inconvenience multiplied across enterprise's thousands of desktops
notes: 64 MB RAM requirement was significant for 1998 enterprise desktop standards

metric_value: NT 5.0 storage management capabilities likely to be a pleasant surprise; one-stop storage shopping and reduced labor burden
notes: Aberdeen rated storage management as a relative positive in the NT 5.0 feature set

metric_value: Benefits will be slow to arrive and unevenly distributed across enterprise types; careful planning is mandatory
notes: Aberdeen's central planning guidance headline

metric_value: Windows 2000 achieved significant enterprise adoption but was largely superseded by Windows XP (Oct 2001) and Server 2003 within 3 years; confirming Aberdeen's prediction of uneven and slow benefits
notes: Consistent with Aberdeen's warning about gradual rather than immediate adoption

metric_value: Microsoft plans 64-bit NT 5.0 for Intel IA-64 when Merced reaches mass market; potentially dramatic performance improvements
notes: Conditional prediction dependent on Itanium mass market penetration

metric_value: IA-64 Itanium never achieved mass market; Intel discontinued Itanium in July 2021; Aberdeen's conditional 64-bit prediction never triggered
notes: Source: Intel discontinued Itanium 2021; platform served only niche HPC/server market

metric_value: Active Directory problems mean mid- and large-sized organizations will need major effort to deploy NT beyond the workgroup
notes: Historically validated; Active Directory had well-documented early-adoption problems

metric_value: NT 5.0 with 40M lines of code and CE integration positions as platform for future of all Windows releases — corporate server to embedded
notes: Aberdeen described this as unprecedented scope for a single OS release

metric_value: NT 5.0 release will occur in frantic atmosphere of Year 2000 concerns and Internet hype; IS should not allow these issues to distract from NT 5.0 migration planning
notes: Aberdeen's strategic warning about competing enterprise IT priorities in late 1998/1999

metric_value: Early implementers who have not preplanned and pretested will face major enterprise-implementation problems similar to NT 3.5.1 and NT 4.0
notes: Aberdeen cited Microsoft's track record of post-FCS bug discovery as basis

metric_value: Systems Management Server 2.0 part of ZAW — requires large ISV customization before yielding value
notes: Aberdeen cautioned against assuming out-of-box zero-administration capability

metric_name: Microsoft Management Console new admin paradigm
metric_value: MMC and Snap-ins represent new administrative paradigm requiring ISV tool development; tools not available at NT 5.0 FCS
notes: Aberdeen predicted ISVs could integrate existing tools relatively easily
